summary:
Last week I had a chance to attend a truly great 2-day tutorial by Philip Schultz and Wilker Aziz. It was held in Mathematikon, Heidelberg and guys really did an amazing job. You can find the slides and a practice notebook on the tutorial’s official github page.
This was my first touch with variatoinal inference and DGMs, so my head was spinning from the start. I tried to keep up as long as I can, finally losing a grip on the second day.
title: I learned something about Variational Inference in NLP

content:
I’ve returned from EMNLP, my first big NLP conference, full of ideas, impressions and inspiration. I went there to present my paper on bipolar disorder prediction on Reddit at WASSA and, of course, to learn as much as I can! You can find my paper here. And yes, my talk went great, thanks for asking.
Here I’d like to share my notes from all the talks I’ve attended. These were initially meant just for me, and I am probably the only one that will read them again :).
Table of Contents:
 Day 1
 Keynote by Julia Hirschberg
 Session 1: Social Applications 1
 Session 2: Social Media
 Session 3: Machine Learning
 Session 4: Language Models
  Day 2
 Session 5: Machine Learning 2
 Session 7: Social Application 2
 Keynote by Gideon Mann
 Session 8: Text Categorization
  Day 3
 Session 9: Sentiment 1 & Machine Learning 3
 Session 10: Discourse
 Session 11: Analyzing Models & Sentiment 2
   DAY 1  Julia Hirschberg Keynote: “Truth or Lie? Spoken Indicators of Deception in Speech”    notes    deceptive speech – deliberate choice to mislead criminals are better than police, judges and similar in detecting it there are acoustic-prosodic and lexical cues system should take individual differences into account: gender, ethinicity… existing dataset: Columbia SRI Colorado Deception corpus (7h of speech, 32 subjects, 70% acc) created a cross-lingual dataset: 340 subjects, 122h of speech, native english speakers questioning native chinese speakers who know english (and vice versa) features: n-grams, LIWC, word embeddings, speech-based (openSMILE, ISO9), gender, native language, NEO-FFIs personality scores, syntactic features Methods: Random Forest, SVM, DL – BiLSTM-lexical + DNN-speech yielding 0.65 F1 Best model: Multi-Task Learning with personality traits 74.4 F1 Significant differences in deceptive speech: Men are loud with higher pich when lying; Woman are just loud, but have jitter when telling the truth Human accuracy: 56.76%    Session 1: Social Applications 1  Privacy-preserving Neural Representations of Text   Coavoux, Narayan, and Cohen    explicit private information can be preprocessed, but implicit (age, authorship) can’t Can attacker guess about input text based on hidden representation? Target private variables: age, gender, named entities that occur in the text attacker eavesdrops while learning the model – attacker beats the baseline for age and gender just based on hidden r defence: double objective (loss_clf = -logP(y|x) - loss_attacker) or adversarial generation (LM) conclusion: latent representations contain a signal for private information code on github    Adversarial Removal of Demographic Attributes from Text Data   Elazar and Goldberg    while learning DeepEmoji attacker eavesdrops –> hich leakage Actively reduce leakage with adversarial setup (Ganing and Lempitsy, 2015) –> remove stuff from representation, but still some leakage Their method: use more layers for adversarial reversal layer; bigger \lambda to reverse with; > 1 adversaries –> they learned diff features still not perfect!    DeClarE: Debunking Fake News and False Claims using Evidence-Aware Deep Learning   Popat, Mukherjee, Yates, and Weikum    prior work: supervised learning (doesn’t use external knowledge) & external sources (require feature modeling and lexicons). Why people don’t like feature modeling any more? :) overcame flaws with: end-to-end NN that takes the external resources into account without human intervention Approach:  external evidence – search engine language style, semantics – BiLSTM interaction claim-external – Attention credibility score based on all of that  beat the baseline on 4 datasets analysis showed clear clusters of Fake and Trusted websites (CNN…)    It’s going to be okay: Measuring Access to Support in Online Communities   Wang and Jurgens    minimal identity cues are important – username that reveals gender or interests (catlady22, pizzamaniac…) support is more than encouragement (14 strategies: validation, advice…) dataset: post-reply from Reddit, Wiki, Stack Exchange goal: rate the reply as: support, polite, agree, offensive most important features are from LIWC –> support in theory constructed massive gender-performance DS from usernames Who recieves support? – woman get more support, but also more disparagement code and data on github    Detecting Gang-Involved Escalation on Social Media Using Context Chang et al.
Session 2: Social Media  Joint Learning for Emotion Classification and Emotion Cause Detection   Chen, Hou, Cheng, and Li    two tasks: (1) emotion classification and (2) emotion cause detection they argue it’s not hard to label cause while you are labeling for emotions joint encoder & linear decoder joint learning improves F1 (is it significant? –> check paper)    Exploring Optimism and Pessimism in Twitter Using Deep Learning   Caragea, Dinu, and Dumitru    they argue there is a difference between sentiment and optimisim/pessimism Dataset: 10k tweets labeled between -3 (most pessimistic) to 3 (most optimistic) when 0 is treshold –> 80% Acc; when (-1) and 1 are –> 90% Acc deep learning architecture different from sentiment: when trained on sentiment dataset, they get poor results. Q: They trained only Stanford analyzer, what when you train a NN like for the main task? verb tense analysis    Predicting News Headline Popularity with Syntactic and Semantic Knowledge Using Multi-Task Learning   Lamprinidis, Hardt, and Hovy    why do you click on a Facebook post? modeling reader’s knowledge – lexical, syntactic, topical Dataset: 82k headlines with click numbers. Then make it a binary classification >50% median are often clicked ones, <50% rarely clicked. Q: Seems kinda random. Auxiliary task: POS tagging GRU-MTL with pretrained embeddings yields better performance than the task alone Q: Why does POS matter for this? How to know which aux task will help? Also, position of a post, image of a post?    Hybrid Neural Attention for Agreement/Disagreement Inference in Online Debates   Chen, Du, Bing, and Xu    does a reply agree or disagree with the comment? two factors:  self-attention –> focuses on important parts inside a comment cross-comment-attention –> reply focused  connect both with something, idk what because they show huge NN for 2 sec :( Attention visualisation & Cross Matrix Product Visualisation –> TODO: Learn how to do that!    Increasing In-Class Similarity by Retrofitting Embeddings with Demographic Information   Hovy and Fornaciari    Homophily? retrofitting – moving the points of distinct classes away from each other (x’, y’) = a(x, y) + b(mean) for test data use translation matrix calculated from the train set benefit in gender and age performance Q: Not different than just learning the embeddings? If you train your embeddings, you’re sepperating classes?    Session 3: Machine Learning  Self-Governing Neural Networks for On-Device Short Text Classification   Ravi and Kozareva    the only presentation I’ve seen with multiple people :) TFLite –> it’s a lite Tensorflow SGNN - projection based NN –> get rid of the embedding layer and use locality sensitive hashing as feature extractor on 42 dialog acts they got SOTA. Q: What if they trained that model without memory/performance restrictions? SOTA on a bunch of other things?    Supervised Domain Enablement Attention for Personalized Domain Classification   Kim and Kim    didn’t catch much    A Deep Neural Network Sentence Level Classification Method with Context Information   Song, Petrak, and Roberts    sucidal classification in medical data –> from a text, predict if a specific sentence expresses suicidal thoughts. BiLSTM + CNN on focus sentences provide right + left context – FOFE (fixed-size ordinally forgetting encoding) reports significant improvements takeaway: context is important (who’d say right :)    Session 4: Language Models  Efficient Contextualized Representation: Language Model Pruning for Sequence Labeling   Liu et al.    ELMo is great, but it contains sufficent information –> prune it w.r.t. the task you’re solving dense connectivity – alows better layer selection via layer-wise dropout sequence labeling with contextualized representations (PPL?) nice experiments, results comparable with SOTA size reduced x10, speed goes up x5-6, Acc down by just 1% integrated & pruned plug-and-play model    DAY 2  Session 5: Machine Learning 2  Deriving Machine Attention from Human Rationales   Bao, Chang, Yu, and Barzilay    they wanna train NN with less data rationales – part of a sentence that directs sentiment –> use them to direct attention Rationale2Attention – transformable from task to task  train R2A on source task use R2A to generate attention for target task train a target classifier with R2A attention  domain-ivariant encoder –> Attention Generator (shared BiLSTM, att.sumarizer) –> MTL (shared BiLSTM, task-specific Attention and MLP) 200 examples + rationales gets the same results as 3100 examples –> cheaper annotation code on github    Semi-Supervised Sequence Modeling with Cross-View Training   Clark, Luong, Manning, and Le    revisit self-training (older method) with NN Blum 1998. idea: 2 models teach each other alternate labeled and unlabeled batches –> how to use it? Cross-View Training – not just exchange predictions like Blum, but use NN shared parameters  2 models (auxiliary models) + Main Predictor Forward LSTM –> Forward prediction; Backward LSTM –> Backward prediction Main Predictor teaches Forward 4 aux tasks  CVT + MTL on 1B unlabeled data create all labels for all tasks on one example 4 sequence tagging, dependency parsing, machine translation significant improvement for CVT + MTL (over ELMo too)    [TACL] Comparing Bayesian Models of Annotation   Paun et al.    probabilistic models of annotation are better than majority vote and similar    Session 7: Social Applications 2  Gideon Mann Keynote: Understanding the News that Moves Markets    notes    we live in a scary world where one headline crashes the stock market some systems react in miliseconds after some article is published (buy/sell stocks)    Session 8: Text Categorization  Zero-shot User Intent Detection via Capsule Neural Networks   Xia et al.    how to handle emerging intents? would be nice if he explained what zero-shot detection and capsules are :D (I managed to figure out for zero-shot, but capsule…note to myself: never assume an audiance knows something)    Hierarchical Neural Networks for Sequential Sentence Classification in Medical Scientific Abstracts   Jin and Szolovits    sequentialy label each sentence in a paragraph (abstract): Background, Methods, Results… contextual sentence features have not been looked into. What is done is CRF which considers context labels they pre-trained word embeddings on scientific papers architecture: embedding layer –> LSTM on sentence level –> LSTM on paragraph level new sota for the task ablation study –> context is the most important    Investigating Capsule Networks with Dynamic Routing for Text Classification   Yang et al.    kinda figured out what a capsule is. Standard CNN kernel output is a scalar, while a capsule outputs a vector (with orientation, length and everything) – somehow this is useful :) they propose a capsule net for text classification    Topic Memory Networks for Short Text Classification   Zeng et al.    topic model – each topic is a distribution of words Neural topic model – generative model put topic memory mechanism on top of NTM, then classifier topic coherence    Few-Shot and Zero-Shot Multi-Label Learning for Structured Label Spaces   Rios and Kavuluru    medical coding – one EMR has 15 codes, 1000 code combinations to chose from graph convolution label embeddings? GCNN over descriptors hierarchy –> concat word vectors and final GCNN layer 2 components: text –> CNN & descriptors –> GCNN conect them with label-wise attention (otherwise, finding relevant subtext would be finding a needle in a haystack) they test few label groups: frequent (> 5 ocurrances), few-shot (>= 1), zero-shot (=0) train jointly, evaluate seperately R@k TODO: read the paper    Sunday  Session 9: Sentiment 1 & Machine Learning 3  Sentiment Classification towards Question-Answering with Hierarchical Matching Network   Shen et al.    BiLSTM to get encoding of a Question and an Answer row-wise Answer-to-Question attention & Question-to-Answer Bidirectional Matching Vectors + self-attention sentence segmentation helps to extract relevant subtext Q&A attentions contribute in both ways dataset on github    Cross-topic Argument Mining from Heterogeneous Sources   Stab et al.    information seeking perspective –> extract pros and cons apply model to heterogeneous texts (8 topics, 25k docs, blogs/news/social media etc.) Labels: pro, con, no-argument 200 random sentences for each topic for calibration (0.72 Kappa) –> 25k sentences with crowdsourcing annotation Bi Contextual LSTM (Gogh et al, 2017) Tried transfer learning & MTL (topic_main & topic_otherdataset) train on 7, test on 1 topic the more you add, the more you get online demo (couldn’t find it)    Summarizing Opinions: Aspect Extraction Meets Sentiment Prediction and They Are Both Weekly Supervised   Angelidis and Lapata    I like the title extract small number of reviews of a product with different aspects and summarize it (and add sentiment) not enought data –> inject prior knowledge with seed words Multi-Seed Aspect Extractor (MATE) – init seed matrices for each aspect (image, sound of a TV…) add MTL Sentiment prediction: multiple instance learning network dataset: aspect labels, opinion salience labels, find summaries TODO: read the paper    Phrase-level Self-Attention Networks for Universal Sentence Encoding   Wu, Wang, Liu, and Ma    pre-trained embedding options:  LM – ELMo, BERT supervised learning – their choice –> smaller model, faster training incorporate syntax into fully-attention network – compose sentence representation on phrased from the dependency tree –> phrase-level self-attention     BanditSum: Extractive Summarization as a Contextual Bandit   Dong et al.    how to select the right sentence to include in a summary? Problem: from continuous NN to discrete (N sent, ROUGE) Sequential Tagging with Reinforcment Learning (proposed before) –> probl: stop sampling once M sentences are selected Approach: treat extractive summarization as contextual Bandit (Context-Arm-Reward)  smaller search space no heuristics needed SOTA     Session 10: Discourse  A Skeleton-Based Model for Promoting Coherence Among Sentences in Narrative Story Generation   Xu et al.    narative story generation task – based on a short input, output a story –> requires tight semantic connection connection among sentences is in key phrases (skeleton):  model the relation among skeletons generate a skeleton, then expand to complete a fluent sentence  Generative module:  input-to-skeleton – seq2seq skeleton-to-target sentence – seq2seq  Skeleton extraction module:  reformulate as a sentence compression problem – seq2seq improve the quality with reinforcement learning. What’s the reward?  Dataset: Visual storytelling –> based on a first sentence, generate rest BLEU +44.8% baseline G-score? Fluency + Coherence    Nexus Network: Connecting the Preceding and the Following in Dialogue Generation   Shen, Su, Li, and Klakow    user –> NLU –> state tracker –> dialog policy –> NLG –> nexus: good response should connect the preceding dialogue context and the followup, not just stand-alone DailyDialog (Li, 2017): 13k conversations, 10 topics new sota najgori talk, a napravili brutalne stvari    A Neural Local Coherence Model for Text Quality Assesment   Mesgar and Strube    local coherence – relatedness of sentence to the next one so far: entity-based relations & lexical relations –> connect Model:  pre-train embeddings LSTM context info find max similarity between LSTM features to model topic of 2 proceeding sentences CNN to extract topic changes output is a vector that represents coherence  DS: 105 articles, which one is more readable? SOTA, but not significant compared to DeCLark Does coherence representation improve Essey Assesment? (ASAP ds on kaggle) –> yes, on esseys that are scored by humans with criteria “Must be logical sequence of events” (narrative prompts) Q: Why only take one word from each sentence with LSTM?    Deep Attentive Sentence Ordering Network   Cui, Li, Chen, and Zhang    sentence ordering task – o = [4, 1, 3, 2]; o’ = [1, 2, 3, 4] RNN models as baseline (previous work) propose self-attention paragraph encoder sentence encoder BiLSTM –> scaled dot product self-attention –> pooling –> decoder –> softmax as coherence probability use random permutation on training 7 different datasets, 3 general topics evaluate tau, accuracy, perfect match ration tons of experiments. SOTA. better in longer texts    Getting to “Hearer-old”: Charting Referring Expressions Across Time   Staliunaite, Rohde, Webber, and Louis    corpus of reffering expressions (Putin, current presindent of Russia) –> When does it stop being neccessary? Reffering expression = name + descriptor (becomes shorter over time, <=1 words –> hearer-old) extracting: NP + NP in syntactic tree 52k entities, 200k+ mentions How long until it becomes hearer-old? Outperforms the MCC baseline    Session 11: Analyzing Models & Sentiment 2  Understanding Deep Learning Performance through an Examination of Test Set Difficulty: A Psychometric Case Study   Lalor, Wu, Munkhadalai, and Yu    psychometric methodology (Item Response Theory for NLP) –> How difficult are certain examples? IRT tests on fully trained models difficulty score is learned from human responses slike koje ne kuzim easy examples are easy; model learns easy ones faster; task-model-item matter github    Lexicosyntactic inference in neural models   White et al.    what type of inference can we model with NN? relation verb - what it’s related to English clause-embedding inference event factuality prediction –> did the action happen? E.g., “Forgot to open doors” –> it didn’t happen verb related DS MegaVerdicality2; evaluate on 3 other DSs current SOTA fails at MV2 –> aren’t robust to variation in lexical and syntactic manner    Modeling Empathy and Distress in Reaction to News Stories   Buechel et al.    prior work – no gold, no psychological background 2 types:  Empathic concern – positive, other-focused Personal distress – negative, self-focused  annotation as psychological experiment – read article, then write text and label (1-7) for both types baseline r=0.4 in Empathic and Distress Best is CNN with FastText    Interpretable Emoji Prediction via Label-Wise Attention LSTMs   Barbieri et al.    attention block for each label BiLSTM –> attention –> linear |s|->1 –> softmax helpful for infrequent labels, inhanced interpretability demo on github
summary:
I’ve returned from EMNLP, my first big NLP conference, full of ideas, impressions and inspiration. I went there to present my paper on bipolar disorder prediction on Reddit at WASSA and, of course, to learn as much as I can! You can find my paper here. And yes, my talk went great, thanks for asking.
Here I’d like to share my notes from all the talks I’ve attended. These were initially meant just for me, and I am probably the only one that will read them again :).
title: Notes from EMNLP 2018

summary: Bipolar disorder, an illness characterized by manic and depressive episodes, affects more than 60 million people worldwide. We present a preliminary study on bipolar disorder prediction from user-generated text on Reddit, which relies on users’ self-reported labels. Our benchmark classifiers for bipolar disorder prediction outperform the baselines and reach accuracy and F1-scores of above 86%. Feature analysis shows interesting differences in language use between users with bipolar disorders and the control group, including differences in the use of emotion-expressive words.
title: Not Just Depressed: Bipolar Disorder Prediction on Reddit

summary: This paper describes our system for the detection of stances in tweets submitted to SemEval 2016 Task 6A. The system uses an ensemble of learning algorithms, fine-tuned using a genetic algorithm. We experiment with various offthe-shelf classifiers and build our model using standard lexical and a number of task-specific features. Our system ranked 3rd among the 19 systems submitted to this task.
title: TakeLab at SemEval-2016 Task 6: Stance Classification in Tweets Using a Genetic Algorithm Based Ensemble

summary: In this paper we describe VERBCROCEAN, a broad-coverage repository of fine-grained semantic relations between Croatian verbs. Adopting the methodology of Chklovski and Pantel (2004) used for acquiring the English VerbOcean, we first acquire semantically related verb pairs from a web corpus hrWaC by relying on distributional similarity of subject-verb-object paths in the dependency trees. We then classify the semantic relations between each pair of verbs as similarity, intensity, antonymy, or happens-before, using a number of manually-constructed lexico-syntatic patterns. We evaluate the quality of the resulting resource on a manually annotated sample of 1000 semantic verb relations. The evaluation revealed that the predictions are most accurate for the similarity relation, and least accurate for the intensity relation. We make available two variants of VERBCROCEAN: a coverage-oriented version, containing about 36k verb pairs at a precision of 41%, and a precision-oriented version containing about 5k verb pairs, at a precision of 56%.
title: VERBCROCEAN: A Repository of Fine-Grained Semantic Verb Relations for Croatian
